nodes.label: Sensory processing fundamentals
nodes.summary: Sensory systems transduce physical/chemical energy into neural signals, encode and threshold them, and adapt over time.

nodes.label: Sensation vs. perception
nodes.summary: Sensation is the detection of stimulus energy; perception is the brain's organization and interpretation of that input.

nodes.label: Sensation
nodes.summary: Sensation is the process by which sensory receptors detect and respond to physical stimulus energy from the environment.

nodes.label: Perception
nodes.summary: Perception is the brain's selection, organization, and interpretation of sensations into a meaningful representation.

nodes.label: Bottom-up (data-driven) processing
nodes.summary: Bottom-up processing builds perception from raw sensory data upward, beginning with the stimulus itself.

nodes.label: Top-down (conceptually-driven) processing
nodes.summary: Top-down processing uses prior knowledge, expectations, and context to interpret incoming sensory data.

nodes.label: Sensory receptors and transduction
nodes.summary: Specialized receptor cells convert specific stimulus modalities into graded membrane potentials and ultimately action potentials.

nodes.label: Sensory receptor
nodes.summary: A sensory receptor is a specialized neuron or cell that responds to a particular form of stimulus energy.

nodes.label: Sensory transduction
nodes.summary: Transduction is the conversion of a physical or chemical stimulus into an electrochemical neural signal.

nodes.label: Receptor (generator) potential
nodes.summary: A receptor potential is the graded depolarization a receptor produces upon transduction; if it reaches threshold it generates action potentials in the sensory neuron.

nodes.label: Photoreceptors
nodes.summary: Photoreceptors are receptors that transduce light energy into neural signals (rods and cones).

nodes.label: Hair cells (mechanoreceptors)
nodes.summary: Hair cells are mechanoreceptors that transduce sound vibration and head movement via stereocilia deflection.

nodes.label: Chemoreceptors
nodes.summary: Chemoreceptors detect chemical stimuli and underlie taste (gustation) and smell (olfaction).

nodes.label: Mechanoreceptors
nodes.summary: Mechanoreceptors respond to pressure, stretch, vibration, and movement of the skin and tissues.

nodes.label: Thermoreceptors
nodes.summary: Thermoreceptors detect changes in temperature (warmth and cold).

nodes.label: Nociceptors
nodes.summary: Nociceptors are free nerve endings that detect noxious (painful) stimuli signaling actual or potential tissue damage.

nodes.label: Osmoreceptors
nodes.summary: Osmoreceptors detect changes in solute concentration (osmolarity) of body fluids.

nodes.label: Baroreceptors
nodes.summary: Baroreceptors are mechanoreceptors that detect blood-pressure (stretch) changes in vessel walls, contributing to interoception.

nodes.label: Sensory pathways and coding
nodes.summary: Sensory information travels along labeled pathways to the cortex, with stimulus features encoded by which neurons fire and how strongly.

nodes.label: Afferent (sensory) neurons
nodes.summary: Afferent neurons carry sensory information from receptors toward the central nervous system.

nodes.label: First-, second-, and third-order neurons
nodes.summary: Most sensory pathways relay through three neurons, with the second- or third-order neuron synapsing in the thalamus en route to cortex.

nodes.label: Thalamus as sensory relay
nodes.summary: The thalamus relays nearly all sensory information (except smell) to the appropriate primary sensory cortex.

nodes.label: Cortical projection (sensory) areas
nodes.summary: Each modality projects to a dedicated primary cortical area that performs initial high-level processing of that sense.

nodes.label: Labeled-line coding
nodes.summary: The modality of a stimulus is encoded by which dedicated pathway carries the signal, regardless of how the line is activated.

nodes.label: Stimulus intensity coding
nodes.summary: Stimulus intensity is encoded by action-potential frequency and by the number of receptors recruited.

nodes.label: Stimulus location coding
nodes.summary: Stimulus location is encoded by which receptors fire and by topographic maps preserved along the pathway.

nodes.label: Receptive field and acuity
nodes.summary: A receptor's receptive field is the region of stimulus space it responds to; smaller, denser fields yield finer spatial acuity.

nodes.label: Thresholds and adaptation
nodes.summary: Detection requires a minimum stimulus, discrimination requires a minimum change, and constant stimuli produce declining responses.

nodes.label: Absolute threshold
nodes.summary: The absolute threshold is the minimum stimulus intensity detectable 50% of the time.

nodes.label: Threshold of conscious perception
nodes.summary: The threshold of conscious perception is the minimum stimulus intensity that reaches awareness, distinct from the lower physiological absolute threshold.

nodes.label: Subliminal stimuli
nodes.summary: Subliminal stimuli fall below the threshold of conscious perception yet may still be processed.

nodes.label: Priming
nodes.summary: Priming is the activation of associations by a stimulus (sometimes subliminal) that biases later perception or response without awareness.

nodes.label: Difference threshold (JND)
nodes.summary: The difference threshold, or just-noticeable difference (JND), is the smallest detectable change between two stimuli.

nodes.label: Sensory adaptation
nodes.summary: Sensory adaptation is the decreased receptor or neural response to a constant, unchanging stimulus over time.

nodes.label: Tonic vs. phasic receptors
nodes.summary: Tonic receptors adapt slowly and signal continuous stimuli, whereas phasic receptors adapt quickly and signal change.

nodes.label: Habituation vs. sensory adaptation
nodes.summary: Sensory adaptation is a receptor-level decline in firing, whereas habituation is a central, learned decrease in behavioral response to a repeated stimulus.

nodes.label: Psychophysics
nodes.summary: Psychophysics quantifies the lawful relationship between physical stimulus magnitude and the resulting subjective sensation.

nodes.label: Weber's law
nodes.summary: Weber's law relates the just-noticeable difference to the magnitude of the original stimulus as a constant proportion.

nodes.label: Weber's law statement
nodes.summary: Weber's law states the JND is a constant proportion of the original stimulus intensity, ΔI/I = k.

nodes.label: Weber fraction (constant)
nodes.summary: The Weber fraction k is the modality-specific constant proportion at which a change becomes just noticeable.

nodes.label: Proportional, not absolute, change
nodes.summary: Larger baseline stimuli require larger absolute changes to be noticed, because detection depends on relative not absolute difference.

nodes.label: Fechner's law
nodes.summary: Fechner's law describes how subjective sensation grows as the logarithm of physical stimulus intensity.

nodes.label: Fechner's law statement
nodes.summary: Fechner's law states perceived sensation magnitude is proportional to the logarithm of stimulus intensity, S = k·log(I).

nodes.label: Derivation from Weber's law
nodes.summary: Fechner derived his logarithmic law by integrating Weber's constant-fraction JNDs as units of subjective magnitude.

nodes.label: Stevens' power law
nodes.summary: Stevens' power law generalizes the stimulus-sensation relationship as a power function fit to magnitude estimation.

nodes.label: Stevens' power law statement
nodes.summary: Stevens' power law states perceived magnitude equals stimulus intensity raised to a modality-specific exponent, S = k·I^a.

nodes.label: Signal detection theory
nodes.summary: Signal detection theory models how observers distinguish a signal from noise using sensitivity and a decision criterion.

nodes.label: Signal vs. noise framework
nodes.summary: Signal detection theory treats detection as discriminating a signal embedded in random background noise.

nodes.label: Hits, misses, false alarms, correct rejections
nodes.summary: Each trial yields one of four outcomes based on stimulus presence and the observer's yes/no response.

nodes.label: Response bias (criterion)
nodes.summary: Response bias is the observer's tendency to say yes or no, shaped by expectations, motivation, and payoffs, independent of sensitivity.

nodes.label: Sensitivity (d-prime)
nodes.summary: Sensitivity reflects the observer's true ability to distinguish signal from noise, separate from decision criterion.

nodes.label: Receiver operating characteristic (ROC)
nodes.summary: The ROC curve plots hit rate against false-alarm rate across criteria to characterize detection performance.

nodes.label: Vision
nodes.summary: The visual system transduces light through the eye and retina and processes it via parallel cortical pathways into perceived images.

nodes.label: Structure of the eye
nodes.summary: Light passes through transparent ocular structures that refract and focus it onto the photoreceptor layer of the retina.

nodes.label: Sclera and fibrous layer
nodes.summary: The sclera is the tough white outer layer of the eye continuous with the transparent cornea; it protects and maintains eye shape.

nodes.label: Cornea
nodes.summary: The cornea is the transparent front surface that provides most of the eye's fixed refractive (light-bending) power.

nodes.label: Pupil and iris
nodes.summary: The iris is the pigmented muscle that adjusts the size of the pupil to control how much light enters.

nodes.label: Pupillary light reflex
nodes.summary: The pupillary light reflex constricts the pupil (via parasympathetic input) in bright light and dilates it (sympathetic) in dim light to regulate light entry.

nodes.label: Lens
nodes.summary: The lens is the adjustable structure that fine-focuses light onto the retina via changes in its curvature.

nodes.label: Accommodation (ciliary muscle)
nodes.summary: Accommodation is the change in lens shape by the ciliary muscle to focus on near versus far objects.

nodes.label: Retina
nodes.summary: The retina is the light-sensitive inner layer of the eye containing photoreceptors and the neural circuitry that begins visual processing.

nodes.label: Fovea
nodes.summary: The fovea is the central retinal pit packed with cones that provides the sharpest, highest-acuity vision.

nodes.label: Macula
nodes.summary: The macula is the cone-rich central region of the retina surrounding the fovea, responsible for detailed central vision.

nodes.label: Optic disc (blind spot)
nodes.summary: The optic disc is where the optic nerve exits the retina, lacking photoreceptors and creating the blind spot.

nodes.label: Aqueous and vitreous humors
nodes.summary: The aqueous and vitreous humors are fluids that maintain eye shape and intraocular pressure and transmit light.

nodes.label: Visual acuity
nodes.summary: Visual acuity is the sharpness of vision, highest at the fovea where cone density and one-to-one ganglion wiring are greatest.

nodes.label: Refractive errors (myopia, hyperopia)
nodes.summary: Myopia (nearsightedness) focuses images in front of the retina and hyperopia (farsightedness) behind it, corrected with lenses.

nodes.label: Photoreceptors: rods and cones
nodes.summary: Rods and cones differ in pigment, sensitivity, distribution, and the type of vision they support.

nodes.label: Rods
nodes.summary: Rods are highly light-sensitive photoreceptors that mediate dim-light (scotopic) and peripheral vision but not color.

nodes.label: Cones
nodes.summary: Cones are photoreceptors concentrated in the fovea that mediate color and high-acuity vision in bright (photopic) light.

nodes.label: Photopic vs. scotopic vision
nodes.summary: Photopic (bright-light, cone-based, color) vision and scotopic (dim-light, rod-based, achromatic) vision describe the two operating ranges of the eye.

nodes.label: Three cone types (S, M, L)
nodes.summary: Three cone classes are maximally sensitive to short (blue), medium (green), and long (red) wavelengths.

nodes.label: Rhodopsin and photopigments
nodes.summary: Rhodopsin (and cone opsins) are photopigments that change shape when struck by light to initiate transduction.

nodes.label: Phototransduction cascade
nodes.summary: Light isomerizes retinal in rhodopsin, triggering a cascade that hyperpolarizes the photoreceptor and reduces glutamate release.

nodes.label: Dark and light adaptation
nodes.summary: The visual system adjusts sensitivity over time when moving between dark and bright environments via pigment regeneration and bleaching.

nodes.label: Retinal neural processing
nodes.summary: Visual signals flow through retinal interneurons that perform contrast enhancement before exiting via the optic nerve.

nodes.label: Bipolar cells
nodes.summary: Bipolar cells relay signals from photoreceptors to ganglion cells in the retinal circuit.

nodes.label: Retinal ganglion cells
nodes.summary: Ganglion cells are the output neurons whose axons form the optic nerve.

nodes.label: Amacrine and horizontal cells
nodes.summary: Amacrine and horizontal cells provide lateral connections that modulate and integrate signals across the retina.

nodes.label: Receptive fields and lateral inhibition
nodes.summary: Center-surround receptive fields with lateral inhibition sharpen edges and enhance contrast.

nodes.label: Central visual pathways
nodes.summary: Optic nerve signals route through the chiasm and thalamus to the occipital cortex and then to parallel processing streams.

nodes.label: Optic nerve and optic chiasm
nodes.summary: At the optic chiasm, nasal retinal fibers cross so each hemisphere processes the contralateral visual field.

nodes.label: Lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)
nodes.summary: The LGN of the thalamus is the relay station for visual information en route to the visual cortex.

nodes.label: Primary visual cortex (V1)
nodes.summary: The primary visual cortex in the occipital lobe performs initial cortical analysis of visual features.

nodes.label: Feature detection
nodes.summary: Feature detectors are cortical neurons that respond to specific stimulus features such as edges, lines, and orientations.

nodes.label: Parallel processing
nodes.summary: Parallel processing analyzes color, form, motion, and depth simultaneously along separate channels.

nodes.label: Dorsal ('where') and ventral ('what') streams
nodes.summary: The dorsal stream processes location and motion while the ventral stream processes object identity.

nodes.label: Visual agnosia and prosopagnosia
nodes.summary: Damage to ventral-stream object-recognition areas can cause visual agnosia (inability to recognize objects) or prosopagnosia (face blindness) despite intact basic vision.

nodes.label: Color vision
nodes.summary: Two complementary theories explain how the visual system encodes color from receptor to cortex.

nodes.label: Trichromatic (Young-Helmholtz) theory
nodes.summary: Trichromatic theory holds that color is encoded by the relative activity of three cone types tuned to red, green, and blue.

nodes.label: Opponent-process theory
nodes.summary: Opponent-process theory holds that color is processed in antagonistic pairs (red-green, blue-yellow, black-white), explaining afterimages.

nodes.label: Color afterimages
nodes.summary: Negative afterimages occur when fatigued opponent channels rebound to the complementary color after prolonged staring.

nodes.label: Color blindness
nodes.summary: Color blindness results from missing or defective cone pigments, most commonly red-green deficiency.

nodes.label: Hearing (audition)
nodes.summary: The auditory system converts sound pressure waves into neural signals encoding pitch, loudness, and location.

nodes.label: Properties of sound
nodes.summary: Sound waves carry frequency, amplitude, and timbre information that map onto perceived pitch, loudness, and quality.

nodes.label: Frequency and pitch
nodes.summary: Frequency (cycles per second, Hz) of a sound wave determines its perceived pitch.

nodes.label: Amplitude and loudness
nodes.summary: Amplitude (intensity) of a sound wave determines its perceived loudness, measured in decibels.

nodes.label: Timbre
nodes.summary: Timbre is the perceived quality of a sound arising from its mix of harmonic frequencies.

nodes.label: Structure of the ear
nodes.summary: The outer, middle, and inner ear progressively collect, amplify, and transduce sound into neural signals.

nodes.label: Outer ear (pinna, auditory canal)
nodes.summary: The pinna and auditory canal collect and funnel sound waves toward the eardrum.

nodes.label: Tympanic membrane (eardrum)
nodes.summary: The tympanic membrane vibrates in response to incoming sound waves, transmitting motion to the ossicles.

nodes.label: Ossicles (malleus, incus, stapes)
nodes.summary: The three middle-ear bones amplify and transmit eardrum vibrations to the oval window of the cochlea.

nodes.label: Eustachian tube
nodes.summary: The Eustachian tube connects the middle ear to the pharynx and equalizes middle-ear pressure with the environment.

nodes.label: Oval window
nodes.summary: The oval window is the membrane where the stapes transmits vibration into the fluid of the cochlea.

nodes.label: Round window
nodes.summary: The round window is the membrane that bulges to relieve pressure as fluid waves travel through the cochlea.

nodes.label: Cochlea
nodes.summary: The cochlea is the fluid-filled spiral structure of the inner ear that houses the organ of hearing.

nodes.label: Endolymph and perilymph
nodes.summary: Endolymph and perilymph are the cochlear fluids whose vibration deflects hair-cell stereocilia to drive transduction.

nodes.label: Basilar membrane
nodes.summary: The basilar membrane vibrates along its length according to sound frequency, supporting tonotopic coding.

nodes.label: Organ of Corti and hair cells
nodes.summary: The organ of Corti sits on the basilar membrane and contains the hair cells that transduce vibration into neural signals.

nodes.label: Pitch perception theories
nodes.summary: Place and temporal codes together explain how the auditory system represents the frequency of a sound.

nodes.label: Place theory
nodes.summary: Place theory holds that pitch is coded by the location along the basilar membrane that vibrates most, best for high frequencies.

nodes.label: Frequency (temporal) theory
nodes.summary: Frequency theory holds that pitch is coded by the firing rate of auditory neurons matching the sound's frequency, best for low frequencies.

nodes.label: Volley principle
nodes.summary: The volley principle holds that groups of neurons fire in alternating volleys to encode frequencies above a single neuron's firing limit.

nodes.label: Tonotopic organization
nodes.summary: Tonotopy is the orderly mapping of sound frequency onto position along the cochlea and auditory cortex.

nodes.label: Auditory pathway and localization
nodes.summary: Auditory signals ascend to the cortex while interaural cues enable localization of sound sources.

nodes.label: Auditory (vestibulocochlear) nerve
nodes.summary: The vestibulocochlear nerve (CN VIII) carries auditory signals from the cochlea toward the brainstem.

nodes.label: Auditory cortex
nodes.summary: The primary auditory cortex in the temporal lobe processes and interprets sound.

nodes.label: Sound localization (interaural cues)
nodes.summary: Sound is localized using interaural time and intensity differences between the two ears.

nodes.label: Conductive vs. sensorineural hearing loss
nodes.summary: Conductive loss results from impaired sound transmission through the outer/middle ear; sensorineural loss results from cochlear hair-cell or auditory-nerve damage.

nodes.label: Other senses
nodes.summary: Somatosensation, taste, smell, and the vestibular and kinesthetic senses provide information about the body and chemical environment.

nodes.label: Somatosensation (touch)
nodes.summary: The skin houses multiple receptor types that detect pressure, temperature, vibration, and pain.

nodes.label: Four touch modalities
nodes.summary: Somatosensation comprises four submodalities: pressure, vibration, temperature, and pain.

nodes.label: Pacinian corpuscles
nodes.summary: Pacinian corpuscles are deep, rapidly adapting receptors that detect deep pressure and vibration.

nodes.label: Meissner corpuscles
nodes.summary: Meissner corpuscles are superficial, rapidly adapting receptors sensitive to light touch and fine detail.

nodes.label: Merkel discs
nodes.summary: Merkel discs are slowly adapting receptors that detect sustained pressure and fine texture.

nodes.label: Ruffini endings
nodes.summary: Ruffini endings are slowly adapting receptors that detect skin stretch.

nodes.label: Free nerve endings
nodes.summary: Free nerve endings are unencapsulated dendrites that detect pain and temperature (warmth and cold).

nodes.label: Two-point discrimination
nodes.summary: Two-point discrimination measures tactile acuity by the minimum separation at which two touches feel distinct, varying with receptor density.

nodes.label: Gate theory of pain
nodes.summary: Gate control theory proposes a spinal 'gate' that can block or allow pain signals from reaching the brain.

nodes.label: Endorphins and pain modulation
nodes.summary: Endogenous opioids (endorphins) and descending pathways can close the pain gate, reducing perceived pain.

nodes.label: Referred and phantom-limb pain
nodes.summary: Referred pain is felt at a site distant from its source; phantom-limb pain is perceived in a missing limb, illustrating central pain processing.

nodes.label: Taste (gustation)
nodes.summary: Taste receptors on the tongue detect dissolved chemicals corresponding to the basic tastes.

nodes.label: Taste buds and receptor cells
nodes.summary: Taste buds contain chemoreceptor cells that detect dissolved tastant molecules.

nodes.label: Five basic tastes
nodes.summary: The five basic tastes are sweet, sour, salty, bitter, and umami.

nodes.label: Papillae
nodes.summary: Papillae are the bumps on the tongue surface that house taste buds.

nodes.label: Flavor (taste-smell integration)
nodes.summary: Flavor is a multimodal percept combining taste, retronasal smell, and texture; much of perceived flavor depends on olfaction.

nodes.label: Taster status and supertasters
nodes.summary: Individual differences in papillae density and bitter-receptor genes produce non-tasters, tasters, and supertasters with heightened sensitivity.

nodes.label: Smell (olfaction)
nodes.summary: Olfactory receptors detect airborne chemicals and project directly to the brain, bypassing the thalamus.

nodes.label: Olfactory receptor neurons
nodes.summary: Olfactory receptor neurons in the nasal epithelium bind odorant molecules to transduce smell.

nodes.label: Olfactory bulb and direct pathway
nodes.summary: Olfactory information projects via the olfactory bulb directly to the cortex and limbic system without a thalamic relay.

nodes.label: Olfaction–limbic memory link
nodes.summary: Direct olfactory projections to the amygdala and hippocampus give smells a strong, immediate link to emotion and memory.

nodes.label: Pheromones
nodes.summary: Pheromones are chemical signals that may influence behavior and physiology of others of the same species.

nodes.label: Vestibular sense
nodes.summary: The vestibular system detects head position and acceleration to maintain balance and spatial orientation.

nodes.label: Semicircular canals
nodes.summary: The three semicircular canals detect rotational (angular) acceleration of the head.

nodes.label: Otolith organs (utricle, saccule)
nodes.summary: The utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration and the head's position relative to gravity.

nodes.label: Balance and equilibrium
nodes.summary: Vestibular signals combine with vision and proprioception to maintain balance and equilibrium.

nodes.label: Vestibulo-ocular reflex
nodes.summary: The vestibulo-ocular reflex stabilizes gaze by driving eye movements opposite to head movement.

nodes.label: Kinesthetic sense (proprioception)
nodes.summary: Proprioception monitors the position and movement of body parts via receptors in muscles and joints.

nodes.label: Proprioception
nodes.summary: Proprioception is the sense of the relative position and movement of one's own body parts.

nodes.label: Muscle spindles and Golgi tendon organs
nodes.summary: Muscle spindles detect muscle stretch and Golgi tendon organs detect muscle tension to inform proprioception.

nodes.label: Kinesthesis vs. vestibular sense
nodes.summary: Kinesthesis senses body-part position/movement from muscle and joint receptors, whereas the vestibular sense tracks whole-head position and acceleration.

nodes.label: Perceptual organization
nodes.summary: The brain organizes sensory input into coherent, stable percepts of objects, depth, motion, and constancy.

nodes.label: Gestalt principles
nodes.summary: Gestalt principles describe how the brain groups elements into organized wholes, where the whole exceeds the sum of its parts.

nodes.label: Gestalt overview ('whole')
nodes.summary: Gestalt psychology holds that we perceive organized wholes rather than isolated parts.

nodes.label: Proximity
nodes.summary: The principle of proximity states that elements near each other are perceived as a group.

nodes.label: Similarity
nodes.summary: The principle of similarity states that elements resembling each other are grouped together.

nodes.label: Continuity (good continuation)
nodes.summary: The principle of continuity states that elements arranged along a smooth line or curve are perceived as continuous.

nodes.label: Closure
nodes.summary: The principle of closure states that we fill in gaps to perceive complete, whole objects.

nodes.label: Symmetry
nodes.summary: The principle of symmetry (prägnanz) states we perceive forms in the simplest, most regular way possible.

nodes.label: Figure-ground
nodes.summary: Figure-ground organization is the perception of objects (figures) as distinct from their background.

nodes.label: Common fate (common motion)
nodes.summary: The principle of common fate states that elements moving together are perceived as a group.

nodes.label: Connectedness and common region
nodes.summary: Elements that are physically connected or enclosed within a common region are perceived as belonging together.

nodes.label: Subjective (illusory) contours
nodes.summary: Subjective contours are perceived edges with no physical boundary (e.g., the Kanizsa triangle), arising from closure-driven completion.

nodes.label: Law of prägnanz
nodes.summary: The law of prägnanz states that ambiguous stimuli are organized into the simplest, most stable form.

nodes.label: Depth perception
nodes.summary: Binocular and monocular cues let the brain reconstruct three-dimensional depth from two-dimensional retinal images.

nodes.label: Binocular cues
nodes.summary: Binocular depth cues require both eyes and are strongest for nearby objects.

nodes.label: Retinal (binocular) disparity
nodes.summary: Retinal disparity is the slight difference between the two eyes' images that the brain uses to compute depth.

nodes.label: Convergence
nodes.summary: Convergence is the inward turning of the eyes for near objects, providing a depth cue from eye-muscle feedback.

nodes.label: Monocular (pictorial) cues
nodes.summary: Monocular cues such as relative size, interposition, linear perspective, and texture gradient provide depth with one eye.

nodes.label: Relative size
nodes.summary: When objects are assumed equal in size, the one casting the smaller retinal image is perceived as farther away.

nodes.label: Interposition (overlap)
nodes.summary: An object that partially blocks another is perceived as closer, providing a monocular depth cue.

nodes.label: Linear perspective
nodes.summary: Parallel lines appear to converge with distance, a monocular cue to depth.

nodes.label: Texture gradient
nodes.summary: Texture appears denser and finer with distance, signaling depth from a single eye.

nodes.label: Relative height
nodes.summary: Objects higher in the visual field (toward the horizon) are perceived as more distant.

nodes.label: Light, shading, and aerial perspective
nodes.summary: Shading conveys form while haze (aerial perspective) makes distant objects appear bluer and blurrier, giving depth.

nodes.label: Motion parallax
nodes.summary: Motion parallax is the depth cue in which near objects appear to move faster than distant ones as the viewer moves.

nodes.label: Perceptual constancy
nodes.summary: The brain perceives objects as stable in size, shape, and color despite changing retinal input.

nodes.label: Size constancy
nodes.summary: Size constancy is perceiving an object as the same size despite changes in its retinal image with distance.

nodes.label: Shape constancy
nodes.summary: Shape constancy is perceiving an object as the same shape despite changes in viewing angle.

nodes.label: Color and brightness constancy
nodes.summary: Color and brightness constancy is perceiving consistent color and lightness despite changes in illumination.

nodes.label: Motion perception
nodes.summary: The brain detects real and apparent motion and can be fooled by motion illusions.

nodes.label: Phi phenomenon / apparent motion
nodes.summary: The phi phenomenon is the illusion of motion created by rapidly successive stationary stimuli.

nodes.label: Stroboscopic motion
nodes.summary: Stroboscopic motion is the perception of continuous movement from a rapid series of slightly different still images, the basis of film.

nodes.label: Autokinetic and induced motion
nodes.summary: The autokinetic effect is apparent movement of a stationary point of light in the dark; induced motion is the illusion that a still object moves because its surroundings move.

nodes.label: Motion aftereffect (waterfall illusion)
nodes.summary: After prolonged viewing of motion in one direction, a stationary scene appears to drift in the opposite direction due to motion-detector adaptation.

nodes.label: Constructive perception and illusions
nodes.summary: Perception is an active construction shaped by expectations, context, and prior experience, sometimes producing errors.

nodes.label: Perceptual set
nodes.summary: A perceptual set is a mental predisposition to perceive one thing rather than another based on expectations and experience.

nodes.label: Context effects
nodes.summary: Context effects occur when surrounding information influences the interpretation of an ambiguous stimulus.

nodes.label: Schemas, culture, and experience
nodes.summary: Prior schemas, cultural background, motivation, and emotion bias top-down interpretation of ambiguous input.

nodes.label: Visual illusions
nodes.summary: Visual illusions are systematic perceptual errors that reveal the brain's interpretive assumptions.

nodes.label: Müller-Lyer illusion
nodes.summary: The Müller-Lyer illusion makes equal lines with inward vs. outward arrowheads appear unequal, attributed to misapplied size-constancy depth cues.

nodes.label: Ponzo and moon illusions
nodes.summary: The Ponzo illusion and the moon illusion both arise when depth/distance cues distort perceived size of equal-retinal-image stimuli.

nodes.label: Attention
nodes.summary: Attention selectively allocates limited perceptual and cognitive resources to some stimuli over others.

nodes.label: Selective attention
nodes.summary: Selective attention filters the flood of sensory input to focus on relevant stimuli.

nodes.label: Selective attention
nodes.summary: Selective attention is the focusing of conscious awareness on a particular stimulus while ignoring others.

nodes.label: Cocktail party effect
nodes.summary: The cocktail party effect is the ability to focus on one voice amid background noise while still detecting salient cues like one's name.

nodes.label: Dichotic listening and shadowing
nodes.summary: In dichotic listening, different messages are played to each ear and the participant shadows one, revealing how little of the unattended channel is processed.

nodes.label: Spotlight model of attention
nodes.summary: The spotlight model treats visual attention as a movable focus that enhances processing of whatever falls within it.

nodes.label: Inattentional blindness
nodes.summary: Inattentional blindness is the failure to notice a fully visible but unexpected stimulus when attention is engaged elsewhere.

nodes.label: Change blindness
nodes.summary: Change blindness is the failure to detect changes in a visual scene when attention is not directed to them.

nodes.label: Filter and resource models
nodes.summary: Bottleneck and resource theories explain how and when unattended information is filtered out.

nodes.label: Broadbent's early-selection filter
nodes.summary: Broadbent's filter model proposes selection occurs early, blocking unattended input before meaning is processed.

nodes.label: Treisman's attenuation model
nodes.summary: Treisman's model proposes unattended input is attenuated rather than fully blocked, allowing salient words through.

nodes.label: Late-selection models
nodes.summary: Late-selection models propose all input is processed for meaning before selection determines what reaches awareness.

nodes.label: Resource (capacity) model
nodes.summary: Resource models treat attention as a limited pool of capacity allocated across competing tasks.

nodes.label: Divided attention and multitasking
nodes.summary: Performing tasks simultaneously is limited by capacity and depends on automaticity and task similarity.

nodes.label: Divided attention
nodes.summary: Divided attention is the allocation of attention across multiple tasks or stimuli at once.

nodes.label: Automatic vs. controlled processing
nodes.summary: Automatic processing requires little attention or effort, whereas controlled processing demands focused conscious attention.

nodes.label: Stroop effect
nodes.summary: The Stroop effect is the slowed naming of ink color when it conflicts with the printed color word, demonstrating automatic reading.

nodes.label: Multitasking and task-switching cost
nodes.summary: Attempting concurrent demanding tasks incurs performance and reaction-time costs from limited capacity and task switching.

nodes.label: Sustained attention (vigilance)
nodes.summary: Sustained attention is the ability to maintain focus on a task over time; performance declines (the vigilance decrement) during prolonged monitoring.

external_prereqs.concept_hint: Resting membrane potential and graded/action potentials of neurons
Thalamus and gross functional organization of the brain
Sound as a longitudinal pressure wave: frequency, wavelength, intensity
Decibel scale and logarithmic measure of sound intensity
Refraction of light at curved surfaces and lens optics
Converging lenses, focal length, and image formation
Diverging vs. converging corrective lenses and focal point shift
Receptor-ligand binding and chemical signal transduction
Cranial nerves and organization of the nervous system
Limbic system: amygdala and hippocampus in emotion and memory
